name: Huginn Muninn
shortDescription: Huginn and Muninn are a pair of ravens that fly all over the world and bring information to the god Odin. They represent the powers of thought and memory.
description: Huginn (Old Norse for "thought") and Muninn (Old Norse for "memory" or "mind") are two ravens that are companions of the god Odin in Norse mythology. They fly all over the world, Midgard, and bring Odin information. Every day, Odin sends them out at dawn to observe the world. They return in the evening, perching on his shoulders and whispering all they have seen and heard into his ear. Odin deeply values their knowledge, and worries greatly about their safety, as evidenced by his lament in the poem Grímnismál: 'Huginn and Muninn fly each day over the spacious earth. I fear for Huginn, that he come not back, yet more anxious am I for Muninn.' The ravens are not merely messengers; they are integral to Odin's wisdom and understanding of the cosmos. Their ability to traverse the realms and gather information highlights the importance of knowledge and perception in Norse cosmology. They are often depicted perched on Odin's shoulders or flying around him, symbolizing his connection to the world and his constant pursuit of wisdom. Their names themselves are symbolic of the cognitive abilities that Odin prizes. They serve as a constant reminder of the importance of thought and memory in maintaining order and understanding in the world. The relationship between Odin and his ravens is a central aspect of his character and his role as a god of wisdom, magic, and knowledge. They are more than just pets or familiars; they are essential components of his power and his connection to the world.
appearance: Two black ravens, often depicted perched on Odin's shoulders or flying around him.

sources.title: The Lost Heroic Cycle of the North
sources.author: Nora Kershaw Chadwick
